name: Milt Hinton
abstract: Milton John Hinton (June 23, 1910 – December 19, 2000) was an American double bassist and photographer. Regarded as the Dean of American jazz bass players, his nicknames included "Sporty" from his years in Chicago, "Fump" from his time on the road with Cab Calloway, and "The Judge" from the 1950s and beyond.

quotes.text: That’s right. I can’t tell you how proud I feel and how grateful. I remember what really made me feel so great, after knowing you all these years, and I was at Carnegie Hall, and I was in a band, and man I felt so good, I’d say, I could remember the time Joe would be there and would say we’re going to play this tune before the singer come out to sing, and I was so glad to be here, for the singer. It was beautiful.
quotes.caption: Milt Hinton recalls playing in a band at Carnegie Hall
quotes.doc_title: Milt Hinton interviewed by Joe Williams, Majesty of the Seas, May 31, 1995

quotes.text: Well I’ve been in Carnegie Hall many a time and I’ve played there and felt very lucky to meet many great people, you know, Streisand and all them. But I’ve never felt like I felt that night cause we’d come, we’d come all the way, I’d seen it all the way. With John Levy you know. His wife Gladys, his first wife Gladys and I graduated from high school together, you know and we were all friends.
quotes.caption: Milt Hinton recalls meeting Streisand and John Levy at Carnegie Hall
quotes.doc_title: Milt Hinton interviewed by Joe Williams, Majesty of the Seas, May 31, 1995

quotes.text: Yeah. Well what happened, there was… a few years ago George Wing decided to give free… two concerts at Carnegie Hall… ask the musicians, this is local New York, if we would play a concert free.
quotes.caption: Recalling George Wein's free Carnegie Hall concerts
quotes.doc_title: Hinton Milt — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

quotes.text: I did her mother‟s Record Of The Year at Carnegie Hall.
quotes.caption: Milt Hinton mentions recording at Carnegie Hall
quotes.doc_title: Hinton Milt — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Delfeayo Marsalis
about_quotes.evidence: When we did Trio Jeepy, which was with Milt Hinton... I started hanging out with Milt Hinton... So 1987, ’88, he said, “I’m going to do a record with Milt Hinton and Tain”
about_quotes.doc_title: Delfeayo Marsalis — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Joe Bushkin
about_quotes.evidence: I was there with Milt Hinton, Buck Clayton, Jo Jones on drums, that was the quartet... Johnny Smith, Jake Hanna and Milt Hinton and myself, we were with Bing on tour.
about_quotes.doc_title: Joe Bushkin interviewed by Monk Rowe, Los Angeles, California, February 15, 1999

about_quotes.speaker: Johnny Varro
about_quotes.evidence: I played with him back in the 50s. ... Well I first played with him and back then we did some recording for Grand Award, the Command label with Pee Wee Irwin.
about_quotes.doc_title: Johnny Varro interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clearwater Beach, Florida, March 16, 2001

about_quotes.speaker: Tom Whaley
about_quotes.evidence: I'm talking with a dear friend, Tom Whaley... and Tom and I have been knowing one another way over 50 years

about_quotes.speaker: Joe Ascione
about_quotes.evidence: When I got to play with Jay McShann and Cab Calloway and Al Hirt and Milt Hinton and all those guys.
about_quotes.doc_title: Joe Ascione interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clearwater Beach, Florida, March 17, 2001

about_quotes.speaker: Lorraine Feather
about_quotes.evidence: The interviewee does not mention Milt Hinton; the mention is made by the interviewer in a question.
